translations.verbatim_quote: From wherever the mind wanders due to its flickering and unsteady nature, one must certainly withdraw it and bring it back under the control of the Self.
From wherever the mind wanders due to its flickering and unsteady nature, one must certainly withdraw it and bring it back under the control of the self.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 677 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The nature of the mind is flickering and unsteady. But a self-realized yogī has to control the mind; the mind should not control him. One who controls the mind (and therefore the senses as well) is called gosvāmī, or svāmī, and one who is controlled by the mind is called go-dāsa, or the servant of the senses.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.26 Wherever the mind, on account of its fickle and unsteady nature, wanders, because of its proclivity to sense-objects, he should, subduing the mind everywhere with effort, bring it under control in order to remain in the self alone by contemplating on the incomparable bliss therein.

disagreements_among_translators.word: niścalati / vaśaṁ nayet (procedure for the wandering mind)

positions.rendering: From wherever the mind wanders due to its flickering and unsteady nature, one must certainly withdraw it and bring it back under the control of the Self
from whatever object the mind goes out — sound, touch, etc. — pulling it back, bring under Self's control by discriminating that all this is unreal
the mind which by its very nature is unsteady — wherever it strays — render it submissive only to the Self

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All traditions read 6.26 as the corrective procedure: when the mind wanders, the practice is to notice (where), restrain (niyamya), and return (ātmani vaśaṁ nayet). Shankara reads the return-instrument as discriminative knowledge (this is unreal); Ramanuja reads it as direct submission of mind to Self. The procedural form is identical across traditions.

literal_meaning: From wherever the unsteady, restless mind wanders away — from there, from there, having restrained it, one should bring it under the control of the Self alone.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 6.26 names the corrective procedure when the gradual restraint of 6.25 fails — the catch-and-return method. The mind by its nature is cañcalam (fickle, oscillating) and asthiram (restless, unstable). Yataḥ yataḥ — wherever — niścalati (it wanders out, slips away), tataḥ tataḥ — from precisely there — niyamya (having restrained), one should bring it ātmani vaśaṁ nayet (back under the Self's control). Shankara: from whatever object the mind goes out due to its fickle nature — sound, touch, etc. — pulling it back from there, one should bring it under the control of the Self by discriminating that all this is unreal. Ramanuja: the mind which by its very nature is unsteady — wherever it strays from concentration — restraining it, render it submissive only to the Self. Engineering analog at strong narrow scope: notice-the-drift, return-to-the-object. The skill named is the noticing-and-returning, not the absence of drift.
